title: White Bound: Nationalists, Antiracists, and the Shared Meanings of Race By Matthew W. Hughey Stanford University Press. 2012. 296 pages. $24.95 paper
abstract: White Bound is a carefully constructed study of the structure and meaning of race, especially whiteness, in the contemporary United States. In an innovative approach, Hughey traces the common logics and similar racial understandings of white racist activists and white antiracist activists to discern the foundational meanings of race in this country. In other words, White Bound uses the extremes to illuminate the normative. The empirical base of this book is an ethnographic study of two organizations, for which pseudonyms are used. National Equality for All (NEA) is the white racist group, headquartered in a declining city in the eastern United States. NEA was founded several decades ago by members of white supremacist, white nationalist, and white conservative groups who feared that the overt racism and confrontational approach of traditional racist groups made it impossible to attract mainstream whites. Instead, it presented a new model of racist politics, one less likely to alienate potential members because it was anchored in a broader rhetoric of identity politics and civil rights. NEA thus positioned itself as an advocate for white rights and racial fairness, arguing that programs and policies that “favor” nonwhites actually undermine racial equity.
